entries.summary: GFDL 1.3 §11 ('Relicensing') was drafted specifically so that 'Massive Multiauthor Collaboration Sites' such as Wikipedia could migrate their content from the GFDL to Creative Commons Attribution-ShareAlike 3.0 (CC-BY-SA). The clause allowed qualifying sites to republish GFDL-licensed MMC content under CC-BY-SA up to a one-time deadline of August 1, 2009. A Wikimedia Foundation Board resolution of 1 December 2007 formally requested the FSF to modify the GFDL accordingly; after the GFDL 1.3 release the Foundation ran a community vote that approved the migration to CC-BY-SA in 2009.

sources.source: FSF — GNU Free Documentation License 1.3 (§11 Relicensing)
sources.excerpt: 11. RELICENSING … 'CC-BY-SA' means the Creative Commons Attribution-Share Alike 3.0 license published by Creative Commons Corporation, a not-for-profit … An MMC is 'eligible for relicensing' if it is licensed under this License … under CC-BY-SA on the same site at any time before August 1, 2009, provided the MMC is eligible for relicensing.

sources.source: Wikimedia Foundation — Resolution: License update (1 Dec 2007)
sources.excerpt: Whereas a long period of discussion and negotiation between and amongst the Free Software Foundation, Creative Commons, the Wikimedia Foundation and others has produced a proposal supported by both the FSF and Creative Commons to modify the Free Documentation License in such a fashion as to allow the possibility for the Wikimedia Foundation to migrate the projects to CC-BY-SA … The Foundation requests that the GNU Free Documentation License be modified in the fashion proposed by the FSF to allow migration by mass collaborative projects to the Creative Commons CC-BY-SA license … Upon the announcement of that relicensing, the Foundation will initiate a process of community discussion and voting before making a final decision on relicensing.

entries.summary: The GFDL allows authors to mark certain sections (Invariant Sections, Cover Texts, Acknowledgements, Dedications) as non-modifiable and non-removable, typically to preserve political statements such as the GNU Manifesto within GNU manuals. This is controversial within the free-software community: in Debian's 2006 General Resolution (vote_001), the project concluded that works with invariant sections conflict with the DFSG's modifiability requirement and are unsuitable for the main distribution, while GFDL works without invariant sections are DFSG-free. Option 2 won the vote.

sources.source: Debian — General Resolution: Why the GNU Free Documentation License is not suitable for Debian main (vote_001, 2006)
sources.excerpt: [GFDL] allows for non-removable, non-modifiable parts to be present in documents licensed under it. Such parts are commonly referred to as 'invariant sections', and are described in Section 4 of the GFDL. As modifiability is a fundamental requirement of the Debian Free Software Guidelines, this restriction is not acceptable for us, and we cannot accept in our distribution works that include such unmodifiable content. … At the same time, we also consider that works licensed under the GNU Free Documentation License that include no invariant sections do fully meet the requirements of the Debian Free Software Guidelines.

entries.summary: The GFDL is intentionally scoped to documentation, not to program source code. The license preamble states that it is designed for manuals for free software and for works whose purpose is instruction or reference, and it explicitly recommends releasing embedded program code in parallel under a separate free software license such as the GPL. Debian's 2006 General Resolution reinforces the point from the other direction: because the GFDL's transparent-copy and invariant-section clauses conflict with the Debian Free Software Guidelines' modifiability requirement, GFDL text cannot be incorporated into free programs and the project urges documentation authors to dual-license under the GPL or a BSD-style license if cross-use with software matters.

sources.source: FSF, GNU Free Documentation License 1.3 (preamble, closing note)
sources.excerpt: We have designed this License in order to use it for manuals for free software, because free software needs free documentation: a free program should come with manuals providing the same freedoms that the software does. But this License is not limited to software manuals; it can be used for any textual work, regardless of subject matter or whether it is published as a printed book. We recommend this License principally for works whose purpose is instruction or reference. ... If your document contains nontrivial examples of program code, we recommend releasing these examples in parallel under your choice of free software license, such as the GNU General Public License, to permit their use in free software.

sources.source: Debian, General Resolution: Why the GNU Free Documentation License is not suitable for Debian main (vote_001, 2006)
sources.excerpt: As a copyleft license, one of the consequences of this is that it is not possible to include content from a document directly into free software under the GFDL. ... GFDL'd documentation is still not free of trouble, even for works with no invariant sections: as an example, it is incompatible with the major free software licenses, which means that GFDL'd text can't be incorporated into free programs. For this reason, we encourage documentation authors to license their works (or dual-license, together with the GFDL) under the same terms as the software they refer to, or any of the traditional free software licenses like the GPL or the BSD license.

sources.source: FSF — Richard Stallman, 'Why publishers should use the GNU FDL'
Debian — General Resolution: Why the GNU Free Documentation License is not suitable for Debian main
